stem: A hospital uses an anti-HCV screening test to screen for hepatitis C. The test has a sensitivity of 93% and a specificity of 81%. The prevalence of hepatitis C in the patient population is estimated to be between 2.9% and 8.1%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has hepatitis C? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(hepatitis C | positive test) ranges from 0.128 to 0.301 as prevalence varies from 2.9% to 8.1%. Point estimate at 5.5% prevalence: 0.222.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.19.
The confidence interval [0.128, 0.301] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (93%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
